description: Scotland's dramatic capital — volcanic rock, medieval wynds, and centuries of stories.

pois.name: Edinburgh Castle
pois.summary: A fortress on volcanic rock, occupied for over 3,000 years — home to Scotland's crown jewels, the famous One O'Clock Gun, and views across the entire city. Every Scottish monarch from the 11th century onwards has had a connection to this rock.

trivia.title: Outlaw King (2018)
trivia.body: Netflix's Robert the Bruce epic used the castle esplanade for key scenes. The real Bruce besieged it in 1314, famously scaling the north face at night.

trivia.title: The One O'Clock Gun
trivia.body: Fired every day at 13:00 (except Sunday) since 1861. Originally a time signal for ships in the Firth of Forth to set their chronometers. Still going.

trivia.title: The Da Vinci Code
trivia.body: Dan Brown's sequel 'The Lost Symbol' weaves Rosslyn Chapel and Edinburgh Castle into a Masonic conspiracy. The castle's Honours of Scotland — Scotland's crown jewels — are very real and on display inside.

trivia.title: The Honours of Scotland
trivia.body: The Scottish Crown Jewels — the Honours of Scotland — are the oldest surviving royal regalia in the British Isles, dating to the 15th and 16th centuries. During the Cromwellian occupation, they were hidden in Kinneff Church, smuggled out in a laundry basket by the wife of the local minister. After the Act of Union in 1707, they were locked in a chest in the castle and forgotten for over a century, until Sir Walter Scott petitioned the Prince Regent to have the chest opened in 1818 — and found them untouched.

trivia.title: Mons Meg
trivia.body: Edinburgh Castle houses Mons Meg — one of the largest cannons ever made, gifted to James II of Scotland by the Duke of Burgundy in 1457. She fired 18-inch stone balls weighing around 150kg, and could hurl them nearly two miles. Ironically, James II had earlier achieved the dubious distinction of becoming the first ruler in history to be killed by his own cannon, when one exploded beside him at the siege of Roxburgh Castle in 1460. Mons Meg herself cracked during a 21-gun salute for the Duke of York in 1681 and never fired again. She was taken to the Tower of London in 1754, returned to Edinburgh by Sir Walter Scott's campaigning in 1829, and has sat in the castle ever since.

pois.name: Arthur's Seat
pois.summary: An ancient volcano rising 251m above the city — arguably the finest urban hike in Europe. The summit gives views across Edinburgh, the Firth of Forth, and on clear days as far as the Highlands, and the whole hill is riddled with history, mystery, and the odd film crew.

trivia.title: Trainspotting & T2 Trainspotting
trivia.body: Arthur's Seat appears most prominently in T2 Trainspotting (2017) — after Renton rescues Spud from the brink, the pair go for an exhilarating run through Holyrood Park and up Arthur's Seat in one of the film's most uplifting sequences. The original Trainspotting (1996) uses the volcano as a skyline backdrop throughout rather than a filming location — the silhouette of Arthur's Seat is woven into Edinburgh's identity in both films. Worth knowing: despite being set entirely in Edinburgh, most of the original Trainspotting was actually filmed in Glasgow, with only the Princes Street opening chase shot in the actual city.

trivia.title: The miniature coffins
trivia.body: In 1836, children found 17 tiny carved coffins tucked in a cliff on Arthur's Seat, each holding a wooden figure in linen. Their origin remains a mystery — possibly effigies for sailors lost at sea, or connected to the Burke and Hare murders. Eight survive in the National Museum.

trivia.title: The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824)
trivia.body: James Hogg's gothic masterpiece is set in the shadow of Arthur's Seat. A pivotal duel takes place on its slopes. It's one of the earliest psychological horror novels ever written.

trivia.title: The pregnant ale-wives' race
trivia.body: In 1661, Edinburgh's Mercurius Caledonius newspaper reported that a dozen pregnant ale-wives had raced up Arthur's Seat 'for a groaning cheese of one hundred pound weight', with second prize being 'a budgell of Dunkeld aquavitae'. The following day the paper announced sixteen fish-wives would trot from Musselburgh to the Canon Cross for twelve pairs of lamb's offal. 17th-century Edinburgh entertainment was nothing if not inventive.

trivia.title: Robert Louis Stevenson & Jekyll and Hyde
trivia.body: Robert Louis Stevenson grew up in Edinburgh with Arthur's Seat visible from his window, and the volcano's brooding presence shaped his imagination. He came to see Edinburgh as fundamentally split — the respectable New Town versus the dark Old Town — a duality he crystallised in The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde (1886). That same idea later inspired Ian Rankin to write his Inspector Rebus series: a detective series that was, underneath, a modern Edinburgh Jekyll and Hyde. Stevenson, like Rankin, used the city's double nature as the engine of his fiction.

pois.name: The Royal Mile
pois.summary: The historic spine of Edinburgh's Old Town, running for exactly one Scottish mile from the Castle down to Holyrood Palace. Every close and wynd branching off it hides centuries of stories — murders, mysteries, literary connections, and the occasional film crew.

trivia.title: Avengers: Infinity War (2018)
trivia.body: Several scenes set in Edinburgh were filmed on and around the Royal Mile. The chase sequence where Scarlet Witch and Vision are attacked by the Black Order used the narrow closes branching off the main street — look out for Cockburn Street in particular.

trivia.title: Burke and Hare
trivia.body: In 1827–28, William Burke and William Hare murdered 16 people in the closes just off the Royal Mile, selling the bodies to anatomist Robert Knox at the nearby medical school. The case led directly to the Anatomy Act of 1832, which reformed how medical schools obtained cadavers.

trivia.title: Harry Potter
trivia.body: J.K. Rowling wrote much of the early Harry Potter books in Edinburgh cafés. The Elephant House on George IV Bridge is the most famous. Greyfriars Kirkyard — just off the Royal Mile — contains gravestones bearing names like Tom Riddle and McGonagall.

trivia.title: Waverley — the only station named after a novel
trivia.body: Edinburgh's main railway station is the only terminus in the world named after a work of fiction — Walter Scott's 1814 novel Waverley. Scott himself largely invented the romantic tartan-clad image of the Highlands: in 1822, he stage-managed King George IV's visit to Edinburgh, persuading the king to wear a kilt in public for the first time. The resulting tartan-mania swept fashionable Britain and permanently shaped how Scotland is seen.

trivia.title: Inspector Rebus & Ian Rankin
trivia.body: The Royal Mile is the beating heart of Ian Rankin's Inspector Rebus novels — Edinburgh's most famous fictional detective has walked these closes in 23 books since 1987. Rankin conceived Rebus as a modern Jekyll and Hyde, the respectable surface of the Old Town concealing a dark underbelly. The fictional Rebus frequents the Oxford Bar on Young Street (a real pub still serving pints today), and Rankin's own handprints are set in the pavement outside the City Chambers on the High Street — the city's award to its most celebrated living author.

pois.name: Palace of Holyroodhouse
pois.summary: The official Scottish residence of the British monarch, at the foot of the Royal Mile — and the scene of some of the most dramatic episodes in Scottish royal history. Mary Queen of Scots lived here through six turbulent years that changed the course of British history.

trivia.title: Mary Queen of Scots
trivia.body: Mary Queen of Scots lived here from 1561 to 1567. Her Italian secretary David Rizzio was stabbed 56 times in her private supper room by conspirators including her husband Lord Darnley — a bloodstain on the floor is still pointed out to visitors today.

trivia.title: Bonnie Prince Charlie
trivia.body: In 1745, Charles Edward Stuart held court here for six weeks during his Jacobite rising, throwing lavish balls while his army occupied the city. The rising ended in disaster at Culloden the following year.

trivia.title: The French poet found under the bed
trivia.body: In 1562, the French court poet Pierre de Boscosel de Chastelard was discovered hiding under Mary Queen of Scots' bed at Holyroodhouse. He had apparently done the same thing once before and been let off with a warning. This time Mary demanded her half-brother the Earl of Moray kill him on the spot. Moray refused, and Chastelard was taken to St Andrews for trial. His defence — that he had been hiding in her privy, not under her bed — cut no ice with the court, and he was beheaded.

pois.name: Greyfriars Kirkyard
pois.summary: One of Edinburgh's most atmospheric graveyards — and one of its most haunted. The kirkyard contains the graves of Covenanters, a famously loyal dog, and names that J.K. Rowling apparently found useful when naming her characters.

trivia.title: Greyfriars Bobby
trivia.body: The Skye Terrier who allegedly guarded his owner's grave for 14 years after his death in 1858. A statue outside the kirkyard gates has been rubbed so often for good luck that its nose is worn smooth.

trivia.title: Harry Potter connections
trivia.body: The kirkyard contains the grave of Thomas Riddell — close to Tom Riddle, Voldemort's real name. There's also a grave for William McGonagall. Whether Rowling drew inspiration here is unconfirmed, but fans flock here regardless.

trivia.title: The Mackenzie Poltergeist
trivia.body: The Black Mausoleum holds George Mackenzie, a judge who persecuted Covenanters in the 1680s. Since a homeless man broke into his tomb in 1999, over 500 people have reported injuries or fainting on ghost tours — one of the most documented alleged poltergeist cases in history.

trivia.title: The Covenanters' Prison
trivia.body: In 1679, over 1,200 Covenanters captured at the Battle of Bothwell Brig were imprisoned in the open air in Greyfriars Kirkyard for five months. Many died of cold and starvation. Their jailer was Sir George Mackenzie — the same 'Bluidy Mackenzie' whose tomb later became associated with poltergeist activity. The walled enclosure in the kirkyard where they were held can still be visited today.

trivia.title: Half-Hangit Maggie
trivia.body: One of Edinburgh's most remarkable stories unfolded near the Grassmarket, just minutes from Greyfriars. In 1724, a fishwife called Margaret Dickson was hanged for concealing the birth of an illegitimate child who died shortly after. Her body was cut down and placed in a coffin for burial — but on the road home, those carrying the coffin heard knocking from inside. Maggie had survived. Under Scots law, having been formally hanged, her sentence was deemed served and she could not be executed again. She returned to her trade, married again, and lived for many more years. Locals called her 'Half-Hangit Maggie'.

pois.name: Victoria Street
pois.summary: A curved, cobbled street of colourful shopfronts widely cited as the inspiration for Diagon Alley in Harry Potter. Built in the 1840s over the older medieval West Bow, it sits above layers of Edinburgh history that go back centuries.

trivia.title: Diagon Alley inspiration
trivia.body: Victoria Street is widely cited as one of the inspirations for Diagon Alley in Harry Potter. Its curving form, tiered layout, and eclectic mix of small specialist shops bear a striking resemblance to the fictional wizarding street.

trivia.title: Built over a buried street
trivia.body: Victoria Street was built in the 1840s over the older West Bow. The lower section still exists below as the Grassmarket — historically the site of public executions, with over 100 witches burned here during the Scottish witch trials.

trivia.title: The boy who nearly burned Edinburgh
trivia.body: In the 16th century, a boy set fire to his father's haystack in a close opposite the Tron Kirk on the Royal Mile — a building that still stands — and nearly burned down the entire Old Town. The chronicler records that he was caught 'most marvellously after escaping out of the town' and was burned at the Cross the following day, 'prompted by the instigation of Satan'. Victoria Street was built over the medieval closes where such events were commonplace.

pois.name: Calton Hill
pois.summary: A hill at the east end of Princes Street with panoramic views and a collection of grand monuments — including one that was never finished. The hill featured prominently in T2 Trainspotting and remains one of the best free viewpoints in any European capital.

trivia.title: Trainspotting 2 (2017)
trivia.body: Calton Hill appears throughout T2 Trainspotting (2017) as one of the film's recurring Edinburgh landmarks — used for establishing shots and atmospheric city views. Danny Boyle filmed extensively across Edinburgh for the sequel, in contrast to the original where most scenes were shot in a Glasgow factory. Over half of T2's scenes were filmed on location in Edinburgh — the sequel is essentially a love letter to the actual city, twenty years on.

trivia.title: Edinburgh's Disgrace
trivia.body: The National Monument was intended as a full replica of the Parthenon, commemorating Scots killed in the Napoleonic Wars. Construction began in 1826 but funds ran out after only 12 columns. It was never completed — mockingly called 'Edinburgh's Disgrace', though locals have since grown fond of its unfinished grandeur.

trivia.title: Scotland's Disgrace and the Parthenon plan
trivia.body: The unfinished National Monument on Calton Hill was meant to be a full replica of the Athenian Parthenon, funded by public subscription to honour Scots killed in the Napoleonic Wars. Construction began in 1826 with great fanfare — but money ran out after just twelve columns. Mocked as 'Edinburgh's Disgrace' and 'Scotland's Shame', the twelve columns were left standing. Locals have since grown fond of the ruin, and several proposals to complete it have been firmly rejected.

pois.name: Mary King's Close
pois.summary: A labyrinth of buried 17th-century streets sealed beneath the Royal Mile since the 18th century — one of Edinburgh's most atmospheric underground attractions. Walking through it gives a genuine sense of the medieval city that still exists beneath the modern one.

trivia.title: The city built over itself
trivia.body: In the 18th century, the Royal Exchange (now the City Chambers) was built directly over a medieval street, sealing the old buildings beneath its foundations. Some residents continued living in the underground levels for decades afterwards. The close takes its name from Mary King, a merchant who owned property here in the 1630s — one of the few women recorded as a business owner in 17th-century Edinburgh.

trivia.title: A Rebus crime scene
trivia.body: Ian Rankin set a pivotal scene in his 1994 novel Mortal Causes in Mary King's Close — a torture victim is discovered in the underground passages at the start of the book. At the time Rankin wrote it, the Close was not yet open to visitors, accessible only by prior arrangement with Edinburgh Council. It has since opened as a major attraction drawing 200,000 visitors a year through the same medieval streets Rebus once treated as a crime scene.

trivia.title: The plague myth
trivia.body: A persistent legend holds that plague victims were walled up alive in Mary King's Close during the 1645 outbreak, sealed in to prevent infection spreading. This is almost certainly false — historical records show the Close continued to be inhabited after the plague. But the story proved irresistible, and the Close acquired a reputation for hauntings long before it was opened to tourists. Ghost tours regularly report unexplained phenomena in the underground chambers.

pois.name: Princes Street
pois.summary: Edinburgh's most famous street — unique in having buildings only on one side, with the other open to the castle and gardens. The opening chase of Trainspotting was filmed here, and it remains the beating commercial heart of the city.

trivia.title: Trainspotting - the opening chase
trivia.body: The iconic opening sequence of Trainspotting (1996) begins here — Renton and Spud sprint down Princes Street after shoplifting from the bookshop at 107-109 Princes Street (now a Next clothing store), with Iggy Pop's Lust for Life blasting over the top. The chase continues down the steps to Calton Road and under the Regent Bridge. Despite the film being set in Edinburgh, this Princes Street sequence and a handful of other shots are almost the only scenes actually filmed in the city — the rest was shot in Glasgow.

trivia.title: The street with one side
trivia.body: Princes Street is unique among major city shopping streets in having buildings only on one side — the south side is entirely open, giving uninterrupted views across Princes Street Gardens to the castle rock. This is not an accident: a legal restriction dating to the early 19th century prevents any building on the garden side. Several attempts to develop it have been defeated in court over the centuries. The result is one of the most dramatic urban vistas in Europe, visible from every café and shop window on the street.

trivia.title: The Scott Monument
trivia.body: The 61-metre Gothic rocket ship that dominates Princes Street Gardens is a monument to the novelist Walter Scott, erected in 1844 — just 12 years after his death. It is the largest monument to a writer anywhere in the world. Visitors can climb 287 steps to the top for views across the city. Scott himself is depicted seated inside with his dog Maida. The monument turns black over time from pollution — it has been cleaned several times but always darkens again.

trivia.title: The city that nearly had a different king
trivia.body: In September 1745, Bonnie Prince Charlie marched down from the north and occupied Edinburgh for six triumphant weeks — holding court at Holyroodhouse and throwing lavish balls while his Highland army camped outside the city walls. He did not enter Edinburgh Castle, which remained in government hands throughout. The city he occupied was the same Princes Street and Old Town you walk today. Within a year his cause would be crushed at Culloden, and he would spend the rest of his life in increasingly dissolute exile in Rome, dying in 1788 never having returned to Scotland.

pois.name: Scottish Parliament
pois.summary: The home of devolved Scottish government at the foot of the Royal Mile — one of the most controversial and celebrated pieces of architecture built in Britain in the last 50 years, designed by a Catalan architect who died before it was finished.

trivia.title: The building that came in at ten times the budget
trivia.body: When the Scottish Parliament building was first mooted in 1997, an early estimate of £40 million was floated. The final cost, when it opened in October 2004, was £414 million — ten times over, and three years late. The architect, Enric Miralles, a Catalan Spaniard who came last in an Evening News public poll during the competition, died of a brain tumour in 2000 aged 45, before seeing a single room completed. His widow carried the project to completion. The building won the RIBA Stirling Prize — the most prestigious architecture award in the UK — in 2005, posthumously vindicating Miralles. It has since also been voted the ugliest building in the world.

trivia.title: Upturned boats and whisky bottles
trivia.body: Miralles designed the building as a meditation on Scotland itself. The distinctive steel roofs of the tower buildings are shaped like upturned fishing boats. The 'trigger panels' around the windows are cut from black Highland granite. The ponds in the grounds represent Scottish lochs. Inside the debating chamber, look for the whisky bottles hidden in the decorative panels. The recurring motif on the exterior panels is believed to be based on Henry Raeburn's famous 'Skating Minister' painting, which hangs in the National Gallery a few hundred metres away. Miralles once said: 'Parliament is just a way of sitting together.'

trivia.title: The Queensberry House cannibal
trivia.body: Incorporated into the parliament complex is Queensberry House, a 17th-century mansion with a remarkable history. In 1707, the Duke of Queensberry — who lived here — was one of the key architects of the Act of Union with England, accused of accepting bribes from Queen Anne to deliver Scottish votes. On the day the Union was signed, his son James — described by contemporaries as a violent idiot of enormous stature — broke free from his room, found a kitchen boy, killed him, and was found roasting him on a spit when discovered. The oven he used can still be seen in the building. Ian Rankin used this story in his Rebus novel Set in Darkness.

pois.name: National Museum of Scotland
pois.summary: The finest collection of Scottish historical artefacts anywhere in the world — from Pictish stones and Jacobite relics to Dolly the sheep — free to enter and one of the most rewarding museums in Britain.

trivia.title: Dolly the sheep
trivia.body: Among the most famous exhibits in the National Museum of Scotland is the taxidermied body of Dolly the sheep — the first mammal ever cloned from an adult cell, created at the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh in 1996. Dolly was named after Dolly Parton, because she was cloned from a mammary gland cell. She lived for six years before being put down in 2003. Her cloning was one of the most significant scientific events of the 20th century and raised profound ethical questions that are still debated today.

trivia.title: The Lewis Chessmen
trivia.body: The museum holds some of the Lewis Chessmen — a set of 12th-century Norse chess pieces carved from walrus ivory and whale teeth, discovered on the Isle of Lewis in 1831. The pieces are among the most recognisable medieval artefacts in the world, and have appeared in Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone as the animated chess pieces in McGonagall's giant chess set. Most of the 93 pieces are in the British Museum in London, which Scotland has long argued should be repatriated. Edinburgh holds 11. The argument continues.

trivia.title: The sword of William Wallace
trivia.body: The museum holds a sword attributed to William Wallace, Scotland's greatest national hero — the man immortalised, somewhat inaccurately, by Mel Gibson in Braveheart. The sword dates to the 16th century, which is about 200 years after Wallace was executed in 1305. This does not deter anyone. Wallace was hanged, drawn, and quartered in London for high treason against a king he had never recognised. Parts of his body were sent to Newcastle, Berwick, Stirling, and Perth as a warning. Scotland made him a national hero anyway.

pois.name: Edinburgh New Town
pois.summary: One of the finest examples of Georgian urban planning in the world — a grid of elegant streets built from 1766 when Edinburgh's overcrowded Old Town was deemed uninhabitable by the wealthy. The contrast with the Old Town across Princes Street is dramatic and deliberate.

trivia.title: Built to escape the smell
trivia.body: In the mid-18th century, Edinburgh's Old Town was one of the most overcrowded places in Europe. Around 50,000 people lived in medieval tenements up to 14 storeys high, sharing wells and outdoor privies. Waste was thrown from windows into the street at 10pm with a shouted warning of 'Gardyloo' — a corruption of the French 'Gardez l'eau'. When plans for the New Town were announced in 1766, the wealthy fled across the drained loch — now Princes Street Gardens — to the new Georgian grid with almost indecent haste. Within a generation the Old Town had become a slum.

trivia.title: Planned by a 23-year-old
trivia.body: The grid of streets that forms the first New Town was designed by James Craig, who was 23 years old when he won the competition in 1766. His plan was elegantly simple: three main east-west streets named for the royal family (George Street in the centre, Queen Street to the north, Princes Street to the south), two garden squares at each end, and linking streets named Hanover, Frederick, and Castle. Craig died in poverty in 1795, having received little credit for the design during his lifetime. The New Town he conceived is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

trivia.title: Deacon Brodie — the real Jekyll and Hyde
trivia.body: The most famous resident of the New Town's fringes was a man who inspired one of literature's great dualities. William Brodie was a respected Edinburgh cabinet maker and town councillor by day — and a burglar by night, using wax impressions of keys made while visiting clients' homes to rob them after dark. He was caught, tried, and hanged in 1788 on a gallows he had himself designed in his capacity as a city councillor. A young Robert Louis Stevenson grew up hearing stories of Brodie, and the idea of a respectable man with a criminal double life became the seed of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde. There is a pub on the Royal Mile named after him.

pois.name: Royal Yacht Britannia
pois.summary: The Queen's floating palace for 44 years — now permanently moored in Leith, Edinburgh's port. Tripadvisor's No.1 UK attraction, and the only place where Queen Elizabeth II said she could truly relax.

trivia.title: The ship that made the Queen cry
trivia.body: HMY Britannia served the Royal Family for 44 years, travelling over one million nautical miles on 968 state visits, stopping at 600 ports in 135 countries — making Elizabeth II the most widely travelled monarch in history. When Britannia was decommissioned in December 1997, the Queen — normally undemonstrative in public — was seen to cry at the ceremony. She had said of the ship: 'This is the only place I can truly relax.' In the event of nuclear war, the Queen and Prince Philip were to be evacuated aboard her to the north-west coast of Scotland. She was built on the Clyde in 1953 and made her last voyage carrying the final Governor of Hong Kong as it was handed back to China in 1997.

trivia.title: The royal honeymoons
trivia.body: Britannia hosted numerous royal honeymoons — Princess Margaret and Antony Armstrong-Jones were the first, in 1960. Princess Anne honeymooned aboard in 1973, and Charles and Diana sailed on her in 1981, departing Gibraltar. The ship's rules required complete silence near the royal apartments, with all work finished by 8am. A thermometer was kept in the Queen's bathroom to ensure the water was always the correct temperature. A mahogany windbreak was added to the balcony deck specifically to prevent breezes from blowing up skirts and inadvertently revealing royal underwear.

pois.name: Grassmarket
pois.summary: A wide cobbled square below the castle where Edinburgh held public executions for centuries — now lined with pubs and restaurants, with a gallows memorial still marking the spot where over 100 Covenanters were hanged.

trivia.title: The public executions
trivia.body: The Grassmarket was Edinburgh's main site for public hangings from the 15th century until 1784. The Last Drop pub — one of several still trading here — takes its name from the scaffold that once stood outside. Landlords rented window seats to spectators for particularly popular executions. Over 100 Covenanters were hanged here in the 1680s for refusing to accept the king's authority over the church — their memorial, a simple flat stone, still sits in the square.

trivia.title: Burke and the anatomy trade
trivia.body: William Burke and William Hare murdered 16 people in the closes nearby between 1827 and 1828, selling the bodies to anatomist Robert Knox at Edinburgh Medical School. Demand for fresh corpses for dissection far exceeded the legal supply — creating a black market for body-snatching and, in Burke and Hare's case, murder. Burke was hanged in January 1829 before a crowd of 25,000. His body was publicly dissected, his skeleton placed on permanent display, and his skin tanned and made into a pocket book — which is still held by the Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh.
